findings.reachability: Authenticated users with the plugin capability (default: manage_options). Attacker crafts a URL to tools.php?page=force-regenerate-thumbnails&goback=https://evil.com and tricks a privileged admin into visiting it; after regen completes the 'Go back' link points to the attacker domain.
findings.justification: The $_GET['goback'] parameter is output directly as a link href using esc_url() which strips javascript: but allows arbitrary HTTPS URLs, enabling open redirect for any admin-accessible page visit.

detection_pattern.section_name: Open Redirect: user-supplied URL echoed via esc_url
detection_pattern.description: Catches places where user-supplied URL parameters are passed through esc_url() and echoed as link destinations without validating the destination is a same-site or allowed URL, enabling open redirect.

findings.reachability: Any plugin or theme that hooks 'regenerate_thumbs_cap' and returns a lower capability (e.g. 'read' or 'edit_posts'). A malicious or vulnerable co-installed plugin could lower the capability, granting subscribers the ability to trigger thumbnail regeneration and bulk deletion of thumbnail files on any attachment.
findings.justification: The capability stored in $this->capability is entirely determined by apply_filters('regenerate_thumbs_cap', 'manage_options') and is used as the sole authorization gate for all AJAX handlers, the admin page form, and the bulk action handler; if a third-party filter returns 'read', any logged-in user can invoke ajax_process_image and cause unlink() calls across the uploads directory.

detection_pattern.section_name: Privilege Escalation: capability via filter without floor
detection_pattern.description: Catches plugins that compute the required capability via a filter with no minimum floor, allowing other plugins to lower the required permission below what is safe for the protected action.

findings.reachability: Any HTTP client that can reach admin-ajax.php with action=regeneratethumbnail (requires no prior auth check before this line).
findings.justification: error_reporting(0) is called on line 584 BEFORE the capability and nonce checks on lines 589 and 592; while this silences PHP errors to protect the JSON response format, it also masks any runtime errors that might otherwise surface security-relevant conditions, and in isolation represents a pre-auth suppression of error reporting that could hide other bugs.

detection_pattern.section_name: Information Exposure: error_reporting(0) before auth check
detection_pattern.description: Catches AJAX handlers that disable error reporting before performing authentication/authorization checks, which can mask PHP errors that would otherwise reveal mishandled inputs or logic errors.

findings.reachability: No direct user input flows into this query; it is called from admin_enqueues() (admin-only page load). Severity is low because there is no user-controlled data in the query.
findings.justification: The query at line 335 uses $wpdb->get_var() with a raw SQL string referencing $wpdb->posts (a WordPress-managed constant), without $wpdb->prepare(), which is a coding-standard violation that could introduce SQLi risk if the query is refactored to include user input in the future.

detection_pattern.description: Catches $wpdb->get_var() calls that directly interpolate a SQL string instead of using $wpdb->prepare(), flagging potential SQL injection paths even when current inputs are safe.
